alma

Chat

The original feature. Send a message, get a response. It looks simple until you try to make it reliable.

How it works

You type a message. The frontend sends it to the Flask backend. The backend calls the Gemini API. The response comes back. The frontend renders it as Markdown.

The conversation array holds every message. Each turn includes the full history so the model has context. The first version lost context between turns because the array was not being passed to the API correctly.

What broke

  • Lost context, the conversation array was not passed to the API. The model responded to each message in isolation. The fix was three lines of code. The debugging took two hours.
  • Textarea focus loss, after sending a message the textarea lost focus. The user had to click back into it before typing the next message. We added a focus call after the send operation completed.
  • Submit button disabled state, the send button was disabled when the textarea was empty. But the disabled check used text.length === 0 which did not account for whitespace-only input. We switched to text.trim().length === 0.
  • Error banner, when the API returned an error the frontend showed a generic "Internal Server Error" with no context. We added an error banner that shows the actual error message from the backend.

The macOS app

The Swift app is the current preferred platform. It uses a NavigationSplitView with a sidebar and detail view. The composer is a simple TextField with a send button. Messages render as Markdown using the MarkdownUI library.

The conversation service manages state with Swift's @Observable macro. It sends messages to the Flask backend, handles the response, and updates the conversation title automatically.

struct ComposerView: View {
    @Binding var text: String
    let onSend: () -> Void
    var isGenerating = false

    var body: some View {
        HStack(alignment: .bottom, spacing: 8) {
            attachmentButton
            textEditor
            sendButton
        }
    }
}

The web version uses React. The Swift version uses SwiftUI. Both talk to the same Flask backend.

Auto-titling

When you send the first message, the conversation title updates to a truncated version of your message. When the backend generates a response, it also generates a title. The Swift app applies the server title if it is not empty and the user has not manually renamed the conversation.

Found something unexpected? Report an issue.
